---
editor:
    render-on-save: true
csl: ieee.csl
format:
  html:
    filters: [tikz.lua, strip-yaml-frontmatter.lua, maintext-filter.lua, sitext-filter.lua]
---

# Beyond shape rigidity: the two-state promoter model {#sec-twostate}

In the preceding sections we developed a family of models centred on
the Negative Binomial (NB) distribution: the NB with shared $p$
(@sec-dirichlet-multinomial), gene-specific $p_g$
(@sec-hierarchical-p), variable capture probability
(@sec-capture-prior), and a zero-inflation gate
(@sec-hierarchical-gate). Despite the flexibility afforded by
gene-specific parameters $r_g$ and $p_g$, the NB constrains every
gene to the same parametric *shape* — its distribution is always
either monotone-decreasing from $0$ or unimodal away from the
boundary, and it can never represent a genuinely bimodal histogram.

For a class of genes that we will call **bursty / bimodal** the NB
fails empirically in two characteristic ways: an excess of zero
counts beyond what any NB can simultaneously fit with a heavy right
tail, and — sometimes — a literal bimodal histogram with one mode
at zero and a second mode at moderate-to-high counts. The physical
origin of these signatures is **slow promoter switching**: when the
promoter's on / off rates are slow relative to mRNA decay, single
cells get caught either in long off stretches (producing near-zero
counts) or in long on stretches (producing Poisson-like counts
around a moderate mean), and the aggregate distribution across
cells has two modes.

In this section we introduce the **non-bursty two-state promoter
model** as a flexible likelihood for these genes, and show that:

1. The non-bursty two-state master equation has a closed-form
   steady-state PMF involving the confluent hypergeometric function
   ${}_1F_1$ — historically the way the model is written, but a
   form we will *not* evaluate numerically.

2. The same distribution has an exact **Poisson-Beta compound
   representation** that avoids special functions and exposes the
   right latent structure for inference.

3. The Poisson-Beta marginal is **closed under binomial thinning**,
   so the model composes with the capture-efficiency machinery
   (@sec-capture-prior) without modification.

4. The Negative Binomial is recovered as a special case in the
   fast-OFF limit ($k^- \gg 1$ relative to mRNA decay). The
   two-state model is therefore a *generalisation* of the NB, not a
   competitor — it adds expressive capacity exactly where the NB
   runs out.

5. The natural rate parameters $(\hat r, k^+, k^-)$ have a
   well-known identifiability ridge, so we reparameterize in terms
   of $(\mu, b, k^-)$ — gene mean, NB-limit mean burst size, and
   OFF rate — under which the priors decouple and "default to NB"
   is encoded by a simple shift of the $k^-$ prior.

6. Three further bijective reparameterizations — the regime
   ratio $s = k^-/k^+$, the mean–Fano coordinates
   $(\mu, F, \kappa)$, and the moment-delta coordinates
   $(\mu, F, \delta)$ with $\delta = 1/(\kappa + 1) \in (0, 1)$
   — leave the likelihood unchanged but rotate the sampled axes
   into directions the data identify approximately
   independently. The mean–Fano variant makes the first two
   observable moments the sampled coordinates and directly bounds
   the posterior-predictive variance per gene; the moment-delta
   variant additionally compresses the unbounded NB-limit ridge
   to a bounded shape coordinate.

7. The marginal log-likelihood is evaluated by **Gauss-Legendre
   quadrature** over the latent ON-fraction, with the Beta density
   evaluated at fixed nodes inside the integrand. Gauss-Jacobi —
   the more elegant choice on paper — is rejected because its
   nodes and weights depend on $(\alpha, \beta)$ through an
   eigendecomposition whose adjoint develops NaN gradients near
   degenerate eigenvalues in the U-shaped Beta regime, exactly
   the bursty regime the two-state model is meant to capture.

## The non-bursty two-state master equation {#sec-twostate-master}

The non-bursty two-state promoter model has a binary promoter state
$s(t) \in \{\text{ON}, \text{OFF}\}$ that switches as

$$
\text{OFF} \xrightarrow{k^+} \text{ON}, \qquad
\text{ON}  \xrightarrow{k^-} \text{OFF},
$$ {#eq-promoter-switching}

an mRNA production rate $r$ (active only in the ON state) and a
first-order degradation rate $\gamma$ for each mRNA molecule. The
joint probability $P_s(m, t)$ that the promoter is in state $s$ and
that the cell contains $m$ mRNAs satisfies a coupled master
equation. Defining the non-dimensional rates
$\hat r = r/\gamma$, $\hat k^+ = k^+/\gamma$,
$\hat k^- = k^-/\gamma$ — all measured in units of the mRNA
lifetime — the steady-state marginal $\pi_{\rm ss}(m) = P_{\rm ON}(m) + P_{\rm OFF}(m)$
has a closed form due to Peccoud and Ycart:

$$
\pi_{\rm ss}(m \mid \hat r, \hat k^+, \hat k^-)
=
\frac{\Gamma(\hat k^+ + m)}{\Gamma(m+1)\,\Gamma(\hat k^+)}
\cdot
\frac{\Gamma(\hat k^+ + \hat k^-)}{\Gamma(\hat k^+ + \hat k^- + m)}
\cdot
\hat r^{\,m}
\cdot
{}_1F_1\!\big(\hat k^+ + m,\; \hat k^+ + \hat k^- + m,\; -\hat r\big),
$$ {#eq-twostate-closed-form}

where ${}_1F_1$ is the confluent hypergeometric function of the
first kind. This is the form most often quoted in the biophysics
literature. We will *not* evaluate it numerically: the series for
${}_1F_1$ has alternating signs in our parameter regime, requires
arbitrary-precision arithmetic to be stable, and is at its most
fragile precisely where the model is most interesting (small $\hat
k^+, \hat k^-$). The Poisson-Beta representation in the next
subsection gives access to the same marginal through a much more
benign numerical route.

## The Poisson-Beta compound representation {#sec-twostate-poisson-beta}

The same steady-state distribution can be written exactly as a
compound:

$$
p \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\hat k^+, \hat k^-),
\qquad
m \mid p \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\hat r\, p).
$$ {#eq-poisson-beta}

That is: the latent variable $p \in [0, 1]$ is drawn from a Beta
distribution whose shape parameters are the non-dimensional
switching rates, and the mRNA count is Poisson with rate
$\hat r\, p$. Marginalizing over $p$ recovers
@eq-twostate-closed-form term by term, but numerically the
compound is what we will use.

**Physical interpretation of $p$.** Direct integration of the
master equation reveals that $p$ is the
*memory-weighted ON fraction* of the promoter,

$$
p(t) = \gamma \int_0^\infty e^{-\gamma\tau}\, \mathbb{1}_{\rm ON}(t - \tau)\, d\tau,
$$ {#eq-on-fraction}

where $\mathbb{1}_{\rm ON}(\cdot)$ is the indicator that the
promoter was ON at the indicated time. The integration kernel is
the exponential mRNA-lifetime distribution; this is why $p$
naturally takes values in $[0, 1]$ and follows a Beta distribution
with the switching rates as shape parameters at steady state. A
fuller derivation is in (@sec-beta-origin); for the present
purpose, $p$ is the *fraction of recent promoter activity weighted
by the mRNA-lifetime kernel*. As we abuse notation throughout this
section by writing $\pi(\cdot)$ for the density of whatever
distribution context makes obvious (cf. @sec-dirichlet-multinomial),
@eq-poisson-beta becomes

$$
\pi(m \mid \hat r, \hat k^+, \hat k^-)
= \int_0^1 \pi(m \mid \hat r\, p)\, \pi(p \mid \hat k^+, \hat k^-)\, dp,
$$ {#eq-poisson-beta-marginal}

with the inner densities Poisson and Beta respectively.

## Closure under binomial thinning {#sec-twostate-thinning}

A single cell does not yield direct observations of $m$. The
sequencing pipeline captures each mRNA molecule independently with
probability $\nu_c$, so the observed UMI count is a binomial
thinning,

$$
u \mid m, \nu_c \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(m, \nu_c).
$$ {#eq-twostate-thinning}

Marginalizing the latent mRNA count via the Poisson-Beta
representation is straightforward: a binomial-of-Poisson is again
Poisson, so

$$
u \mid p, \nu_c \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\hat r\, p\, \nu_c).
$$ {#eq-twostate-thinning-collapsed}

Equivalently in PGF form (or in the closed-form ${}_1F_1$ marginal
of @eq-twostate-closed-form), the substitution
$z \mapsto 1 - \nu_c(1 - z)$ leaves the functional shape of the
distribution invariant and simply rescales the production rate
$\hat r \mapsto \nu_c \hat r$. **The two-state distribution is
closed under binomial thinning.** The capture probability $\nu_c$
plays the same multiplicative role here that it plays for the NB
(@sec-capture-prior): it is absorbed into an effective per-gene,
per-cell rate, and inference machinery written for the bare model
extends to the thinned model without modification.

The generative model we will use throughout the rest of the section
is therefore the *thinned* Poisson-Beta:

$$
p_{gc} \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\hat k^+_g, \hat k^-_g),
\qquad
u_{gc} \mid p_{gc} \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\hat r_g\, p_{gc}\, \nu_c),
$$ {#eq-twostate-thinned-poisson-beta}

with per-gene rates $(\hat r_g, \hat k^+_g, \hat k^-_g)$ and per-cell
capture $\nu_c$.

## The NB limit {#sec-twostate-nb-limit}

The two-state distribution reduces to the Negative Binomial when
the OFF rate is fast compared to mRNA decay, $\hat k^- \gg 1$, with
$\hat k^+$ and $\hat r$ remaining finite. Intuitively: the
promoter spends short bursts ON and long stretches OFF, with the
mRNA lifetime acting as a low-pass filter that averages over the
fast switching. In this limit, the Beta on the latent $p$
concentrates near the origin while keeping the product
$\hat r\, p$ at the right scale, and the marginal collapses to

$$
\pi_{\rm ss}(m) \;\xrightarrow{\hat k^- \gg 1}\;
\mathrm{NegBinom}\!\big(m \;\big|\; \hat k^+,\; 1/(1 + \hat r/\hat k^-)\big).
$$ {#eq-nb-limit}

The effective NB shape is $\hat k^+$ — the *ON rate* — and the
effective NB burst size is the ratio
$\hat r/\hat k^- = $ production-rate / OFF-rate, which is exactly
the **mean number of mRNAs produced per ON visit**. So the NB
parameters that scribe's NB-family models infer can be read as
biophysical quantities of an underlying two-state process whenever
the fast-switching limit applies.

The contrapositive is what motivates the present extension: genes
the NB fits poorly are precisely those for which the data
*reject* the fast-switching limit, and require the full two-state
machinery.

## Identifiability and the $(\mu, b, k^-)$ reparameterization {#sec-twostate-reparam}

The natural parameters $(\hat r, \hat k^+, \hat k^-)$ are not all
identifiable from data. In the NB-like regime ($\hat k^- \gg
\hat k^+$) the data constrain only two combinations of the three —
the mean and the Fano factor — leaving a one-dimensional ridge
along which the likelihood is approximately flat. Concretely:
simultaneously scaling
$\hat r \to \lambda \hat r$ and $\hat k^+ \to \lambda \hat k^+$
leaves the mean fixed, and to leading order in
$\hat k^+/\hat k^-$ also leaves the Fano factor fixed. NUTS or any
sampler that visits the ridge will produce a banana-shaped
posterior, and any reported summaries of $(\hat r, \hat k^+,
\hat k^-)$ in the NB-like regime are misleading.

A clean fix is to **reparameterize in terms of the well-identified
combinations.** We sample three positive scalars per gene:

$$
\mu_g \,=\, \text{gene mean expression},
\qquad
b_g \,=\, \text{mean burst size (NB-limit)},
\qquad
k^-_g \,=\, \text{OFF rate},
$$ {#eq-twostate-natural-params}

with the natural-parameter quantities derived as

$$
k^+_g \,=\, \mu_g / b_g,
\quad
\alpha_g \,=\, k^+_g,
\quad
\beta_g \,=\, k^-_g,
\quad
\hat r_g \,=\, \mu_g + b_g\, k^-_g.
$$ {#eq-twostate-derived}

This map is **mean-preserving by construction**:

$$
\langle u_{gc}\rangle
\,=\, \hat r_g \cdot \frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g + \beta_g}
\,=\, (\mu_g + b_g\, k^-_g) \cdot \frac{\mu_g / b_g}{\mu_g / b_g + k^-_g}
\,=\, \mu_g,
$$ {#eq-mean-preservation}

independently of $b_g$ and $k^-_g$. The reparameterization thus
splits the identification cleanly: $\mu_g$ is anchored by the data
mean, $b_g$ is interpretable as the NB-limit burst size (and is
identified through the higher moments / tail behavior), and
$k^-_g$ controls how far the gene departs from the NB limit (large
$k^-_g$ recovers NB; moderate $k^-_g$ admits bimodality).

**Prior recommendations.** A natural choice in unconstrained space
is Normal-on-each-parameter with a softplus transform to the
positive real line. We use, by default:

$$
\log \mu_g \,\sim\, \mathcal{N}(0,\, 3^2),
\qquad
\log b_g \,\sim\, \mathcal{N}(0,\, 1.5^2),
\qquad
\log k^-_g \,\sim\, \mathcal{N}(3,\, 2^2),
$$ {#eq-twostate-priors}

(reading $\log$ here as the inverse of softplus, not exp; the
quantitative difference is small for values away from 0).

The shift of the $k^-$ prior toward a moderately large value
encodes a **mild NB tilt**: under softplus, $\mathcal{N}(3, 2^2)$
puts most posterior mass on $k^-_g \in [\sim 0.55, \sim 6.3]$,
which spans the bursty regime ($k^- \lesssim 1$) and an NB-like
edge ($k^- \gtrsim 5$). Genes the NB fits well are pushed by the
likelihood into the upper end; bursty genes are pulled by the
likelihood toward the lower end. This is intentionally a *mild*
prior — strong NB defaults are available by tightening the
$k^-$ prior, or by switching the transform to a heavier-tailed
log-normal, but at the cost of making it harder for the data to
overcome the prior when a gene genuinely is bursty.

## Alternative reparameterizations {#sec-twostate-altreparam}

The map @eq-twostate-derived eliminates the identifiability ridge by
making $\mu_g$ the *sampled* mean — a quantity the data anchor
directly — and demotes $b_g$ and $k^-_g$ to derived shape variables.
This is a substantial improvement over the bare $(\hat r,\,
\hat k^+,\, \hat k^-)$ coordinates, but it is not the end of the
story when inference is done by **mean-field variational inference
(VI)**. Mean-field approximates the posterior by an independent
product

$$
q(\mu_g,\, b_g,\, k^-_g)
\;\approx\;
q(\mu_g)\, q(b_g)\, q(k^-_g),
$$ {#eq-meanfield-product}

with each marginal $q(\cdot)$ a transformed Gaussian in
unconstrained space. The Kullback-Leibler divergence
$\mathrm{KL}(q\,\|\,\pi)$ being minimised is, at fixed marginals,
penalised by every direction of *posterior correlation* the product
form cannot represent. The quality of mean-field therefore depends
not just on whether the parameters are identifiable but on whether
they are *approximately uncorrelated in the posterior*. Two
parameterizations that yield the same likelihood can give very
different mean-field approximations: the one whose coordinates
align with the directions the data inform independently will
produce tighter posteriors and, in turn, tighter posterior
predictive distributions.

This subsection introduces two reparameterizations that target
this geometric question. Both are exact bijections of
@eq-twostate-derived — the likelihood, the NB limit, and the
binomial-thinning closure are unchanged. What changes is which
*axes of the posterior* the variational guide is asked to
represent.

### The regime ratio $s = k^-/k^+$ {#sec-twostate-ratio}

The Negative Binomial limit @eq-nb-limit is reached not at large
$k^-_g$ alone but at large $k^-_g$ *relative to* $k^+_g$ — when
the promoter spends a vanishing fraction $k^+/(k^++k^-)$ of its
time ON. The natural ratio

$$
s_g
\;=\;
\frac{k^-_g}{k^+_g}
\;=\;
\frac{b_g\, k^-_g}{\mu_g}
$$ {#eq-switching-ratio}

is dimensionless, scale-invariant across genes, and directly
measures how far a gene sits from the NB limit ($s_g \to \infty$
gives NB, $s_g$ moderate gives bursty). A gene that is "NB-like"
at $\mu = 5$ and a gene that is "NB-like" at $\mu = 500$ share the
same characteristic $s$; their absolute $k^-$ values differ by an
order of magnitude.

Sampling $(\mu_g,\, b_g,\, s_g)$ in place of $(\mu_g,\, b_g,\,
k^-_g)$ recovers the natural-parameter quantities via

$$
\alpha_g \,=\, k^+_g \,=\, \mu_g / b_g,
\quad
\beta_g \,=\, k^-_g \,=\, s_g\, k^+_g \,=\, s_g\, \mu_g / b_g,
\quad
\hat r_g \,=\, \mu_g\, (1 + s_g),
$$ {#eq-twostate-ratio-derived}

and the mean is preserved identically:

$$
\langle u_{gc}\rangle
\,=\, \hat r_g \cdot \frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g + \beta_g}
\,=\, \mu_g\,(1+s_g) \cdot \frac{k^+_g}{k^+_g + s_g\,k^+_g}
\,=\, \mu_g.
$$ {#eq-mean-preservation-ratio}

The geometric improvement is that mean-field on
$(\log\mu,\, \log b,\, \log s)$ no longer asks the guide to
discover a curved manifold along which independent draws of $b$
and $k^-$ must conspire to keep the regime ratio sensible. The
data informs $s_g$ approximately independently of $\mu_g$ and
$b_g$ — a gene's regime classification (NB vs bursty) is
essentially scale-free — so the product form @eq-meanfield-product
written in $(\mu, b, s)$ coordinates is a much closer fit to the
true posterior than the same form written in
$(\mu, b, k^-)$ coordinates. The NB-limit prior tilt that
@eq-twostate-priors places on $\log k^-$ shifts naturally to the
regime axis: $\log s_g \sim \mathcal{N}(3, 2^2)$ encodes the same
"mildly favour NB" preference but does so in a scale-invariant way
that applies uniformly across high- and low-expression genes.

### The mean and Fano factor as sampled coordinates {#sec-twostate-mean-fano}

The regime ratio addresses one geometric coupling but not the only
one. The posterior-predictive *variance* of a gene under
@eq-twostate-thinned-poisson-beta is

$$
\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}]
\,=\, \langle u_{gc}\rangle
\,+\, \hat r_g^{\,2}\, \mathrm{Var}[p_{gc}]
\,=\, \mu_g \,+\, \hat r_g^{\,2} \cdot
\frac{\alpha_g\,\beta_g}{(\alpha_g+\beta_g)^2\,(\alpha_g+\beta_g+1)},
$$ {#eq-twostate-variance}

a function of all three parameters. Substituting any of the
foregoing parameterizations gives a *nonlinear* relationship
between the sampled coordinates and the implied variance. In
$(\mu, b, k^-)$ coordinates,

$$
\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}] - \mu_g
\,=\,
\frac{b_g^{\,2}\, \mu_g\, k^-_g}{\mu_g + b_g\, k^-_g + b_g};
$$ {#eq-twostate-excess-var-mubk}

in $(\mu, b, s)$ coordinates an analogous expression holds with
$k^-_g$ replaced by $s_g \mu_g/b_g$. Independent mean-field draws
of two coordinates that *both* enter the variance multiplicatively
combine into an implied variance whose distribution is much
wider than the true posterior on $\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}]$ —
*exactly* the failure mode that surfaces as posterior-predictive
bands too wide to match observed empirical distributions.

The fix at the parameterization level is to make the variance
itself one of the sampled coordinates. Define

$$
F_g
\,=\,
\frac{\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}]}{\langle u_{gc}\rangle} - 1
\;\geq\; 0,
\qquad
\kappa_g \,=\, \alpha_g + \beta_g,
$$ {#eq-twostate-mean-fano-defs}

the **excess Fano factor** and the **Beta concentration**.
Sampling $(\mu_g,\, F_g,\, \kappa_g)$ in unconstrained-positive
space, the natural-parameter quantities are derived as

$$
\alpha_g \,=\,
\frac{\kappa_g\, \mu_g}{\mu_g + F_g\,(\kappa_g + 1)},
\qquad
\beta_g \,=\,
\frac{\kappa_g\, F_g\,(\kappa_g + 1)}{\mu_g + F_g\,(\kappa_g + 1)},
\qquad
\hat r_g \,=\, \mu_g + F_g\,(\kappa_g + 1).
$$ {#eq-twostate-mean-fano-derived}

A short verification confirms that *both* the first two moments
are preserved by construction:

$$
\langle u_{gc}\rangle
\,=\, \hat r_g \cdot \frac{\alpha_g}{\alpha_g + \beta_g}
\,=\, \mu_g,
\qquad
\frac{\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}]}{\langle u_{gc}\rangle} - 1
\,=\, F_g,
$$ {#eq-mean-fano-preserved}

independently of all other parameters. The variational guide is
now asked to factorize across coordinates that *each* control a
distinct, data-anchored quantity: $\mu_g$ sets location, $F_g$
sets predictive variance, and $\kappa_g$ sets the latent-Beta
shape — equivalently, the gene's departure from the NB limit.

The NB limit reads cleanly in these coordinates as $\kappa_g \to
\infty$. From @eq-twostate-mean-fano-derived,

$$
\alpha_g \,\to\, \mu_g / F_g,
\qquad
\beta_g \,\to\, \kappa_g,
\qquad
\hat r_g \,\to\, F_g\, \kappa_g,
$$ {#eq-mean-fano-nb-limit}

and the two-state distribution collapses to an NB with shape
$\mu_g / F_g$ and burst size $F_g$. Two consequences:

1. In the NB limit, the **NB burst size equals the excess Fano
   factor**. This is the well-known identity
   $\mathrm{Var}/\mu = 1 + \mu/r_{\mathrm{NB}}$ written in a more
   directly observable form. The single variable $F_g$ carries the
   information the NB encoded in its dispersion parameter.

2. The "default to NB" tilt now goes on $\kappa_g$, *not* on $F_g$:
   a strong NB-default prior is $\log \kappa_g \sim \mathcal{N}(c,
   \sigma^2)$ with $c$ large. The prior on $F_g$ is left broad
   because the data informs the predictive variance directly.

This is the two-state analog of the NB-family mean–odds
strategy used elsewhere in the pipeline: sample directly the
quantities the data anchor through low-order moments, and demote
the remaining freedom to a shape coordinate whose prior carries
the regime preference. There, the mean and the mean-odds ratio
$\phi = (1-p)/p$ are the sampled coordinates and $r$ is derived;
here, the mean and the excess Fano factor are the sampled
coordinates and the latent-Beta concentration is the shape knob.
In both cases the variational guide's product form factorizes
along axes the data informs approximately independently.

### Compressing the NB-limit ridge: $\delta = 1/(\kappa + 1)$ {#sec-twostate-moment-delta}

The mean-Fano parameterization removes the geometric coupling
between predictive variance and gene magnitude, but leaves one
operational nuisance: the NB limit is $\kappa \to \infty$, an
unbounded direction. Under a mean-field $\log$-Normal guide on
$\kappa$, the variational posterior is forced to allocate
probability mass over the entire positive real line, and for
NB-like genes the data does not localize $\kappa$ — the
posterior just inherits the prior's tail. That is mass spent
representing irrelevant variation in a parameter the data only
distinguishes up to "large."

A natural fix is to map the unbounded $\kappa \geq 0$ to a
bounded coordinate. The substitution

$$
\delta_g \,=\, \frac{1}{\kappa_g + 1}
\;\in\; (0, 1)
$$ {#eq-delta-defn}

is well-suited: $\delta_g \to 0$ corresponds to the NB limit
($\kappa_g \to \infty$), $\delta_g \to 1$ corresponds to the
extreme bursty regime ($\kappa_g \to 0$, the Beta degenerates to
a delta at $\{0, 1\}$), and every value in between is a
well-defined two-state shape. Sampling $\delta_g$ via a
logit-Normal $\operatorname{logit}\delta_g \sim
\mathcal{N}(\ell, \sigma^2)$ — equivalently $\delta_g =
\operatorname{sigmoid}(\ell + \sigma z)$ for $z \sim
\mathcal{N}(0, 1)$ — gives a smooth, bounded variational
distribution over the entire shape range.

Inverting @eq-delta-defn as $\kappa_g = (1 - \delta_g)/\delta_g$
and substituting into @eq-twostate-mean-fano-derived gives the
$(\mu, F, \delta)$ forward map:

$$
\alpha_g \,=\, \frac{\mu_g\,(1 - \delta_g)}{\mu_g\, \delta_g + F_g},
\qquad
\beta_g \,=\, \frac{F_g\,(1 - \delta_g)}{\delta_g\,(\mu_g\, \delta_g + F_g)},
\qquad
\hat r_g \,=\, \frac{\mu_g\, \delta_g + F_g}{\delta_g}.
$$ {#eq-moment-delta-derived}

A direct verification (or applying the chain rule to
@eq-mean-fano-preserved) confirms that the first two moments are
preserved by construction in the new coordinates as well:

$$
\langle u_{gc}\rangle \,=\, \mu_g,
\qquad
\frac{\mathrm{Var}[u_{gc}]}{\langle u_{gc}\rangle} - 1 \,=\, F_g.
$$ {#eq-moment-delta-moments-preserved}

The NB limit is now compressed into a *boundary* of the
parameter space rather than scattered across an unbounded ridge.
Taking $\delta_g \to 0$ in @eq-moment-delta-derived,

$$
\alpha_g \,\to\, \mu_g / F_g,
\qquad
\beta_g \,\to\, \infty,
\qquad
\hat r_g \,\to\, F_g / \delta_g \,\to\, \infty,
$$ {#eq-moment-delta-nb-limit}

so the same NB(shape = $\mu/F$, burst = $F$) limit is recovered,
but accessed through a single coordinate $\delta_g \to 0$ rather
than the full $\kappa_g \to \infty$ axis. The "default to NB"
prior tilt becomes a left-shift on $\operatorname{logit}\delta$:
$\operatorname{logit}\delta_g \sim \mathcal{N}(\ell, \sigma^2)$
with $\ell$ negative puts most mass at small $\delta$, hence at
large $\kappa$, hence near the NB limit. Concretely
$\operatorname{logit}\delta_g \sim \mathcal{N}(-4, 4)$ gives
median $\delta_g \approx 0.018$, corresponding to $\kappa_g
\approx 55$ — moderately NB-like — with a substantial tail
toward larger $\delta$ admitting bursty genes.

The operational difference from $(\mu, F, \kappa)$ is that the
mean-field guide on $(\log\mu, \log F, \operatorname{logit}
\delta)$ no longer wastes mass on the unbounded ridge. For
NB-like genes the data localizes $\delta$ near 0 and $q(\delta)$
sits on a tight, bounded region; for bursty genes the data pulls
$\delta$ toward moderate values and $q(\delta)$ widens within
$(0, 1)$. The variational support exactly matches the support of
the data's identifying information.

### Choosing among the four parameterizations {#sec-twostate-altreparam-choice}

All four parameterizations describe the same family of
distributions; they differ only in which axes of the posterior
mean-field VI is asked to represent independently.

- $(\mu, b, k^-)$ is the physics-natural choice: $b$ is the
  NB-limit mean burst size, $k^-$ is the OFF rate
  non-dimensionalized by mRNA decay. Recommended when the
  analyst wants the most direct biophysical interpretation and
  is using posterior sampling (NUTS) that recovers correlations
  exactly.

- $(\mu, b, s)$ replaces the absolute OFF rate by the
  dimensionless regime ratio $s = k^-/k^+$. The "NB-ness" of a
  gene is now a single scalar, scale-invariant across genes, and
  the variational guide does not have to learn a curved manifold
  along which $b$ and $k^-$ co-vary to keep $s$ sensible.
  Recommended when fitting many genes with widely varying $\mu$
  by mean-field VI.

- $(\mu, F, \kappa)$ makes the first two observable moments the
  sampled coordinates. Posterior uncertainty in $F$ directly
  controls the width of the posterior predictive distribution
  per gene; uncertainty in $\kappa$ controls only the shape of
  the latent Beta. Recommended when posterior-predictive bands
  under the natural or ratio parameterizations are
  systematically wider than the observed gene-level variance — a
  signature of mean-field smearing across the implied-variance
  manifold.

- $(\mu, F, \delta)$ replaces $\kappa$ by the bounded
  $\delta = 1/(\kappa+1) \in (0, 1)$. Same first-two-moment
  guarantees as $(\mu, F, \kappa)$, but the NB-limit ridge is
  compressed to a boundary rather than an unbounded direction.
  Recommended when $(\mu, F, \kappa)$ fits but with a posterior
  on $\kappa$ that visibly tracks its prior — i.e. when the data
  classifies a gene as "NB-like" without identifying *how* far
  into the NB regime it sits.

The four are mathematically equivalent; the distinction is
operational, governed by how mean-field's independence assumption
interacts with the data's identifiability structure. An analyst
running NUTS does not need to choose: any of the four will
recover the same posterior up to MCMC noise. An analyst running
SVI should match the parameterization to the failure mode the
posterior-predictive checks expose.

## Inference: Gauss-Legendre quadrature instead of ${}_1F_1$ {#sec-twostate-quadrature}

The per-observation marginal log-likelihood, derived from the
thinned Poisson-Beta @eq-twostate-thinned-poisson-beta, is

$$
\log \mathcal{L}(u_{gc} \mid \mu_g, b_g, k^-_g, \nu_c)
\,=\,
\log \int_0^1 \mathrm{Poisson}\!\big(u_{gc} \,\big|\, \hat r_g\, p\, \nu_c\big)
\cdot
\mathrm{Beta}\!\big(p \,\big|\, \alpha_g, \beta_g\big)\, dp,
$$ {#eq-twostate-loglik}

with $(\alpha_g, \beta_g, \hat r_g)$ as in @eq-twostate-derived.
The integrand is a smooth, strictly positive function of
$p \in [0, 1]$, and the integral is a one-dimensional definite
integral against a fixed reference measure. Two quadrature
strategies present themselves for this integrand.

### Two possible quadratures {#sec-twostate-quad-options}

**Option A: absorb the Beta into the quadrature weight (Gauss-
Jacobi).** Map $[0,1]$ to $[-1,1]$ via $p = (1+x)/2$ and match
the Beta weight to the standard Jacobi weight $(1-x)^a (1+x)^b$
with $a = \beta - 1$ and $b = \alpha - 1$:

$$
\int_0^1 g(p) \cdot \mathrm{Beta}(p \mid \alpha, \beta)\, dp
\,\approx\,
\sum_{k=1}^K \tilde w_k\, g(p_k),
$$ {#eq-gauss-jacobi-beta}

where the nodes $p_k$ and weights $\tilde w_k$ depend on
$(\alpha, \beta)$. Computing them at fit time requires solving
the eigenvalue problem of the tridiagonal matrix encoding the
Jacobi three-term recurrence (the Golub-Welsch construction).
This is exact through polynomial degree $2K-1$ for the smooth
factor $g$ and has the appealing property that the difficult
boundary behavior of the Beta — vanishing at $0, 1$ for
$\alpha, \beta > 1$, diverging there for $\alpha, \beta < 1$ — is
absorbed into the weight rather than the integrand.

**Option B: keep a fixed quadrature and evaluate the Beta at
nodes (Gauss-Legendre).** Use the standard Gauss-Legendre rule on
$[0,1]$ (uniform reference measure) and write

$$
\int_0^1 f(p)\, dp
\,\approx\,
\sum_{k=1}^K w_k\, f(p_k),
$$ {#eq-gauss-legendre-01}

with $f(p) = \mathrm{Poisson}(u_{gc} \mid \hat r_g\, p\, \nu_c)
\cdot \mathrm{Beta}(p \mid \alpha_g, \beta_g)$. The nodes $p_k$
and weights $w_k$ depend only on $K$ — *not* on
$(\alpha, \beta)$ — so they are computed once at the start of
inference and cached. The Beta density is evaluated at the fixed
nodes inside the integrand.

### Why we use Gauss-Legendre {#sec-twostate-quad-choice}

Mathematically the two options are equivalent at infinite $K$. At
finite $K$ Gauss-Jacobi is more efficient *per node* because it
matches the integrand's reference measure. But under
automatic-differentiation, where gradients flow back from the
log-likelihood through nodes and weights into the upstream
$(\alpha, \beta)$, the two options behave very differently.

The Golub-Welsch eigendecomposition is differentiable in
exact arithmetic, but the implicit-differentiation formula for
eigenvectors of a symmetric matrix involves the reciprocal of
*differences* between eigenvalues:

$$
\frac{\partial v_i}{\partial \theta}
\,\propto\,
\sum_{j \neq i}
\frac{v_j^{\!\top} (\partial M / \partial \theta) v_i}{\lambda_i - \lambda_j}\, v_j,
$$ {#eq-eigh-gradient}

where $(M, \lambda_i, v_i)$ is the tridiagonal Jacobi matrix
and its eigenpairs and $\theta$ stands for $\alpha$ or $\beta$.
The Jacobi recurrence coefficients depend smoothly on
$(\alpha, \beta)$, but for ill-conditioned regimes —
specifically the U-shaped Beta with $\alpha, \beta < 1$, exactly
the bursty regime the two-state model is meant to capture —
neighbouring eigenvalues of the truncated tridiagonal matrix can
get arbitrarily close. Floating-point evaluation of
@eq-eigh-gradient with $\lambda_i \approx \lambda_j$ produces NaN
gradients even when the forward eigendecomposition gives a finite
loss. In practice the forward log-likelihood is finite; the
backward pass is what blows up.

Gauss-Legendre sidesteps this entirely. Its nodes and weights
are *constants*: no autodiff path runs through an
eigendecomposition. The Beta density `Beta(p_k | α, β)` is a
plain, autodiff-friendly function of $(\alpha, \beta)$ at fixed
$p_k$:

$$
\log \mathrm{Beta}(p_k \mid \alpha, \beta)
\,=\,
(\alpha - 1)\log p_k
\,+\, (\beta - 1)\log(1 - p_k)
\,-\, \log B(\alpha, \beta),
$$ {#eq-beta-logpdf-at-node}

with $\log B(\alpha, \beta) = \log\Gamma(\alpha) +
\log\Gamma(\beta) - \log\Gamma(\alpha + \beta)$. Every term is
differentiable with no eigenvalue gap to worry about. The price
is more variation of the integrand at the boundaries (since the
Beta singularity is no longer absorbed by the weight), and
correspondingly a larger $K$ to maintain accuracy in the U-shaped
regime. For the parameter ranges encountered in single-cell data
the trade is worth taking: a robust autodiff path is more
valuable than a smaller node count.

### Log-sum-exp evaluation {#sec-twostate-loglik-logsumexp}

We evaluate @eq-twostate-loglik via the log-sum-exp identity

$$
\log \mathcal{L}(u_{gc} \mid \cdots)
\,=\,
\operatorname*{logsumexp}_{k=1, \ldots, K}
\!\left[
\log w_k
\,+\, \log \mathrm{Beta}(p_k \mid \alpha_g, \beta_g)
\,+\, \log \mathrm{Poisson}\!\big(u_{gc} \,\big|\, \hat r_g\, p_k\, \nu_c\big)
\right],
$$ {#eq-twostate-loglik-quad}

with the inner log-Poisson computed in log-rate form
($\log \mathrm{Poisson}(u \mid \lambda) = u\log\lambda - \lambda
- \log u!$ with $\log\lambda = \log\hat r_g + \log p_k +
\log\nu_c$) to keep both overflow at large rates and underflow at
small $p_k$ from biting. The $u = 0$ case is handled separately
to avoid the IEEE-754 trap $0 \cdot (-\infty) = \mathrm{NaN}$
that arises from $u \cdot \log p_k$ when $p_k$ underflows.

The choice $K \in \{40, \ldots, 80\}$ is sufficient for UMI
counts $u_{gc}$ up to several hundred. The Poisson factor has
effective polynomial degree on the order of $u_{gc}$, and the
Gauss-Legendre rule is exact through degree $2K-1$ for the
combined Beta·Poisson integrand against the uniform weight. For
exceptional very-high-mean genes the quadrature can be augmented
with a Gaussian approximation of the Poisson kernel at the modal
location of $p$, but this is an optional refinement that the
implementer should profile before adding.

## Integration with the existing pipeline {#sec-twostate-integration}

Three properties make the integration with the rest of scribe's
pipeline frictionless.

1. **Capture efficiency is unchanged.** The thinning closure
   @eq-twostate-thinning-collapsed means the per-cell capture
   probability $\nu_c$ enters the two-state likelihood by
   multiplying the rate, exactly as it enters the NB likelihood in
   @sec-capture-prior. The same priors, the same hierarchical
   structures, the same biology-informed anchors all carry over.

2. **The parameter set per gene grows from $(r_g, p_g)$ to
   $(\mu_g, b_g, k^-_g)$.** Posterior summaries gain a per-gene
   $k^-_g$ that flags bursty genes: posteriors concentrated above
   some threshold are NB-like; posteriors near or below $1$ are
   genuinely bursty and would not have been fit by the NB. The
   derived $\alpha_g = \mu_g/b_g$ and $b_g$ are NB-limit-natural
   quantities — readers used to the NB family can compare them
   directly to the NB shape and burst size from the fast-switching
   limit @eq-nb-limit.

3. **Compositional inference / differential expression** is the
   one place that needs more care. The shared-$p$ Dirichlet-
   multinomial factorization that makes NBDM such a clean
   compositional model (@sec-dirichlet-multinomial) relies on a
   special algebraic property of the Gamma distribution
   (independent Gammas with a shared rate normalize to a
   Dirichlet) that the Beta does not share. The per-cell
   composition $\rho_g = \hat r_g\, p_g / \sum_j \hat r_j\, p_j$
   is still a well-defined random vector on the simplex, and a
   sample-based CLR-DE pipeline still applies — but the
   closed-form Dirichlet step disappears. A full development of
   the corresponding sample-based DE workflow is forward work; see
   the broader DE plan for the projected design.

## Bayesian denoising {#sec-twostate-denoising}

The NB-family denoiser exploits Poisson–Gamma conjugacy to obtain a
closed-form posterior for the true molecule count $m_{gc}$ given the
observed UMI count $u_{gc}$.  The two-state model replaces the Gamma
latent with a Beta latent $p_{gc}$, which breaks conjugacy but yields
an equally clean quadrature solution.  We develop both the posterior
mean and a sampling algorithm below.

### Problem setup

Recall the generative model with binomial capture
(@sec-twostate-thinning):

$$\begin{aligned}
p_{gc} &\sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_g, \beta_g),\\
m_{gc} \mid p_{gc} &\sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\hat r_g\, p_{gc}),\\
u_{gc} \mid m_{gc},\, \nu_c &\sim \mathrm{Binomial}(m_{gc}, \nu_c).
\end{aligned}$${#eq-ts-denoise-gen}

By the Poisson thinning property, the observed and *dropped* counts
$d_{gc} = m_{gc} - u_{gc}$ are conditionally independent given
$p_{gc}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
u_{gc} \mid p_{gc},\, \nu_c &\sim
  \mathrm{Poisson}(\nu_c\, \hat r_g\, p_{gc}),\\
d_{gc} \mid p_{gc},\, \nu_c &\sim
  \mathrm{Poisson}\bigl((1-\nu_c)\, \hat r_g\, p_{gc}\bigr).
\end{aligned}$${#eq-ts-denoise-thin}

The denoising problem is: given the observed count $u_{gc}$ and the
model parameters $(\alpha_g, \beta_g, \hat r_g, \nu_c)$, compute
$\langle m_{gc} \mid u_{gc}\rangle$ or draw samples from
$\pi(m_{gc} \mid u_{gc})$.

### Posterior of the latent ON-fraction

By Bayes' rule, the posterior of $p_{gc}$ given the observation is

$$
\pi(p \mid u) \propto
  \mathrm{Poisson}(u \mid \nu\hat r\, p)
  \;\cdot\;
  \mathrm{Beta}(p \mid \alpha, \beta),
$${#eq-ts-denoise-posterior}

where we drop cell/gene subscripts for clarity.
Expanding, the unnormalized log-posterior is

$$
\log\tilde\pi(p \mid u) =
  (\alpha - 1)\log p + (\beta - 1)\log(1-p)
  - \log B(\alpha,\beta)
  + u\log(\nu\hat r\, p) - \nu\hat r\, p
  - \log\Gamma(u+1).
$${#eq-ts-denoise-logpost}

The exponential tilt $e^{-\nu\hat r\, p}$ from the Poisson likelihood
prevents the posterior from being a standard Beta.  We therefore
evaluate it numerically using the same Gauss–Legendre quadrature
infrastructure developed for the marginal log-likelihood in
@sec-twostate-quadrature.

Given $K$ Gauss–Legendre nodes $\{p_k\}_{k=1}^K$ and weights
$\{w_k\}_{k=1}^K$ on $[0,1]$, the unnormalized posterior weight at
each node is

$$
\tilde\pi_k \;=\; w_k \cdot
  \mathrm{Beta}(p_k \mid \alpha,\beta)
  \cdot
  \mathrm{Poisson}(u \mid \nu\hat r\, p_k),
$${#eq-ts-denoise-weights}

and the normalizing constant is $Z = \sum_k \tilde\pi_k$.

### Posterior mean of true counts

Since $m = u + d$ and $d \mid p \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\bigl(
(1-\nu)\hat r\, p\bigr)$, we have

$$
\langle m \mid u \rangle
  = u + \langle d \mid u \rangle
  = u + (1-\nu)\,\hat r\;\langle p \mid u \rangle,
$${#eq-ts-denoise-mean}

where the posterior mean of the latent ON-fraction is approximated by

$$
\langle p \mid u \rangle
  \;\approx\;
  \sum_{k=1}^K p_k \;\frac{\tilde\pi_k}{Z}.
$${#eq-ts-denoise-Ep}

### Posterior variance of true counts

Since $u$ is fixed, $\mathrm{Var}(m \mid u) = \mathrm{Var}(d \mid u)$.
Applying the law of total variance over $p$:

$$
\mathrm{Var}(d \mid u) =
  \bigl\langle \mathrm{Var}(d \mid u, p) \bigr\rangle_{\!p|u}
  + \mathrm{Var}_{p|u}\!\bigl(\langle d \mid u, p \rangle\bigr).
$${#eq-ts-denoise-ltv}

The within-variance is
$\mathrm{Var}(d \mid u, p) = (1-\nu)\hat r\, p$ (Poisson), and the
conditional mean is $\langle d \mid u, p\rangle = (1-\nu)\hat r\, p$.
Therefore

$$
\mathrm{Var}(m \mid u) =
  (1-\nu)\,\hat r\;\langle p \mid u \rangle
  + \bigl[(1-\nu)\,\hat r\bigr]^2\,\mathrm{Var}(p \mid u),
$${#eq-ts-denoise-var}

where $\mathrm{Var}(p \mid u)
= \langle p^2 \mid u\rangle - \langle p \mid u\rangle^2$ and
$\langle p^2 \mid u\rangle \approx \sum_k p_k^2\, \tilde\pi_k / Z$.

### Sampling from the posterior of true counts

To draw samples of $m_{gc}$, we use an ancestral approach:

1. **Sample $p^*$**: for each $(c,g)$, draw from the discrete
   categorical distribution defined by $\{\tilde\pi_k / Z\}$ over the
   quadrature grid (or a finer uniform grid with inverse-CDF
   inversion).
2. **Sample $d^*$**: draw
   $d^* \sim \mathrm{Poisson}\bigl((1-\nu_c)\,\hat r_g\, p^*\bigr)$.
3. **Compute $m^*$**: set $m^* = u_{gc} + d^*$.

This produces samples that reflect the full posterior uncertainty,
including the unobserved promoter activity.

### Recovery of the NB limit

When both $\alpha_g,\, \beta_g \gg 1$ (the NB limit; see
@sec-twostate-nb-limit), the Beta prior concentrates around its
mean $\alpha/(\alpha + \beta)$, the posterior
$\pi(p \mid u)$ becomes a sharp peak, and

$$
\langle p \mid u \rangle
  \;\to\;
  \frac{\alpha}{\alpha + \beta},
$$

so that
$\langle m \mid u \rangle \to u + (1-\nu)\hat r \cdot
\alpha/(\alpha+\beta) = u + (1-\nu)\mu$,
recovering the NB denoising formula.  This continuity ensures that
the two-state denoiser gracefully reduces to the NB denoiser for
genes that do not exhibit promoter switching.

## Practical recommendations {#sec-twostate-practical}

The two-state likelihood is most useful as a *targeted refinement*
of the NB family rather than a wholesale replacement. Concrete
recommendations:

- **Default**: keep the NB family for analyses where a per-gene NB
  fit is adequate. The two-state machinery is more expensive (one
  quadrature sum per observation) and only purchases extra
  flexibility on bursty / bimodal genes.

- **Switch to two-state when** a posterior predictive check on
  the NB shows a systematic mismatch you cannot fix with NB
  parameter changes: an excess of zeros next to a heavy right
  tail, or a literal bimodal histogram. For those genes, the
  two-state $k^-_g$ posterior tells you whether the bimodality is
  real (posterior of $k^-_g$ concentrated near or below 1) or an
  artefact of model misspecification elsewhere (posterior of
  $k^-_g$ pushed up against the prior near a large value).

- **Default-to-NB priors.** The $k^-$ prior in
  @eq-twostate-priors is a mild lean toward the NB limit; the
  data dominate the prior whenever the bursty signature is
  present. Analysts wanting a stronger NB default can tighten the
  $k^-$ prior; analysts wanting a flat prior can broaden it.
  Either is a one-line change in the prior specification; the
  model itself is unchanged.

- **Validation plan.** A reasonable rollout is: (i) synthetic
  data with known $(\mu, b, k^-)$ per gene to confirm parameter
  recovery, (ii) a curated subset of ~50 real genes split between
  NB-easy and NB-poor-fit cases to confirm the two-state model
  matches the NB on the easy genes and improves the fit on the
  hard ones, (iii) the full dataset with a per-gene comparison of
  posterior predictive ECDFs.

# Cross-gene correlation structure {#sec-twostate-cross-gene}

The independent-gene two-state model of @sec-twostate succeeds on
per-gene shape and moment recovery — it captures bursty,
bimodal, and over-dispersed distributions that the NB family
structurally cannot match. But "per-gene" is the operative
caveat: every gene is fit in isolation, with no mechanism for
the model to learn that genes co-vary across cells. Yet
single-cell RNA-seq data is overwhelmingly correlated across
genes — TFs co-regulate co-expression modules, chromatin state
perturbs blocks of co-located genes, regulons co-vary by
construction. Independent-gene models throw all of this
structure away.

This section extends the two-state likelihood with a per-cell
**Gaussian latent** ``z_c`` that perturbs the per-gene
production rate multiplicatively — adding cross-gene correlated
structure on top of the per-gene two-state shape. The
extension is the **TwoState-LogNormal-Rate** model (TSLN-Rate),
the structural analogue of NB-LogNormal (@sec-nb-lognormal)
applied to the Poisson–Beta compound likelihood instead of the
Negative Binomial.

We will show:

(i)   The TSLN-Rate generative model.  Per-cell ``z_c ~ N(0, Σ)``
      with low-rank ``Σ = WW^T + diag(d)``; per-(cell, gene)
      Poisson rate ``λ_cg = r_hat_g · exp(z_cg − η_c)``; counts
      drawn from the same Poisson–Beta compound as
      @sec-twostate, only with ``r_hat_g`` replaced by the
      ``z``-modulated ``λ_cg``.

(ii)  Per-cell Laplace marginalization. The Gaussian latent
      ``z_c`` is integrated out via a Laplace approximation
      around its MAP. Closed-form gradients and a defensively
      clamped Hessian-diagonal make the inner Newton solve fast
      and stable.

(iii) The NB-LogNormal limit. As ``β → ∞`` with ``α = r`` fixed,
      the TSLN-Rate factors collapse to NBLN's ``a_g = (u+r)
      p(1-p)`` form exactly. The new model nests NBLN as a
      limiting case.

(iv)  Gauge analysis. A rigid-translation gauge between
      ``log r_hat_g`` and ``z_cg`` is identified softly by the
      Gaussian prior on ``z``; structurally broken by a
      hard-cascade freeze on the gene-level positives.

(v)   Practical recommendations. When to use TSLN-Rate vs the
      independent-gene two-state model, how to read the new
      result fields, and the cross-gene covariance signal that
      verifies the latent ``z`` is genuinely picking up
      cross-gene structure.

## TSLN-Rate generative model {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-model}

Sample, per gene ``g``, the three positive parameters of the
two-state model as before
(@eq-twostate-natural-priors):

$$
\mu_g \sim \pi_{\mu},\quad
b_g \sim \pi_{b},\quad
k^-_g \sim \pi_{k^-},
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-gene-priors}

with the natural-parameterization derivations

$$
\alpha_g = \mu_g / b_g,\quad
\beta_g = k^-_g,\quad
\hat r_g = \mu_g + b_g k^-_g.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-derived}

(@_twostate_reparam in the implementation also applies a
mean-preserving floor to keep ``α`` and ``β`` away from the
Jacobi-fragile regime.)

Now, **per cell ``c``**, sample a Gaussian latent:

$$
z_c \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma),\quad
\Sigma = W W^\top + \mathrm{diag}(d),
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-latent-prior}

with low-rank loadings $W \in \mathbb{R}^{G \times k}$ and
diagonal residual variance $d \in \mathbb{R}^G_{>0}$. The
per-(cell, gene) Poisson rate becomes:

$$
\lambda_{cg} = \hat r_g \cdot \exp(z_{cg} - \eta_c),
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-lambda}

where ``η_c`` is the (optional) per-cell capture offset (matching
@sec-capture-prior; absent when capture is not modelled). The
observation is then a Poisson–Beta compound, the same as in
@sec-twostate but with this $z$-modulated rate:

$$
p_{cg} \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_g, \beta_g),\quad
u_{cg} \mid p_{cg}, z_c \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda_{cg}\, p_{cg}).
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-obs}

### Why ``z`` on the production rate {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-why-rate}

There is more than one place we could put a Gaussian latent on
top of the two-state model:

1. **On the production rate** ``r_hat_g``, as above. Equivalent
   to ``log r_hat_cg = log r_hat_g + z_cg``. Mean responds
   multiplicatively.

2. On the activation log-odds
   ``η_act_g = log(k^+_g / k^-_g)``. Mean responds via the
   sigmoid — saturating, with a hard ceiling at ``r_hat_g``.

Option 1 (TSLN-Rate) is mathematically the cleaner cascade
analog of NB-LogNormal: the latent enters the Poisson rate
multiplicatively, so in the NB limit (``β → ∞``) the
marginal becomes NB-LogNormal exactly. Option 2 (TSLN-Logit)
has a stronger biophysical interpretation (TF and chromatin
shifts as Boltzmann free-energy perturbations on the activation
free energy ``ΔG_act = -k_B T \log(k^+/k^-)``) and a saturating
mean response. Option 2 is treated separately in a follow-up
section.

This section develops Option 1 in detail. The cleanest test that
``z`` is doing real work is the cross-gene covariance signal of
@sec-twostate-tsln-rate-practical, which is non-zero whenever
``Σ`` has non-zero off-diagonal entries.

## Per-cell Laplace marginalization {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-laplace}

Integrating ``z_c`` out of the joint density exactly is
intractable — there is no closed-form marginal for a Gaussian
mixed against a Poisson–Beta compound. We use a Laplace
approximation around the per-cell MAP:

$$
\log p(u_c \mid \Theta) \approx \log p(u_c, z_c^\ast \mid \Theta)
- \tfrac{1}{2} \log\det(-H_c) + \tfrac{G}{2}\log 2\pi,
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-laplace-elbo}

where $z_c^\ast$ is the MAP of $\log p(u_c, z_c \mid \Theta)$
under the Gaussian prior and $H_c$ is the Hessian of that
log-density at $z_c^\ast$. The Laplace approximation is exact
to the extent that the per-cell log-posterior is well-approximated
by a Gaussian near its mode — which holds when the data is
informative enough to constrain $z_c$ tightly.

### Per-cell joint log-density

Let $\Theta = (\mu, b, k^-, W, d, \eta)$ collect the globals.
Substituting @eq-tsln-rate-lambda and @eq-tsln-rate-latent-prior
into the joint:

$$
\log p(u_c, z_c \mid \Theta)
= \sum_g \log \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{PB}}(u_{cg} \mid \alpha_g, \beta_g, \hat r_g e^{z_{cg} - \eta_c})
\;-\;\tfrac{1}{2} z_c^\top \Sigma^{-1} z_c
\;+\;\text{constants}.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-joint}

The first term is a sum of Poisson–Beta marginal log-likelihoods
(@eq-twostate-pb-marginal) at the $z$-modulated rate; the second
is the Gaussian prior on $z_c$. We use the latent-coordinate
convention ``x_c ≡ z_c + log r_hat`` so the prior on $x_c$
becomes $\mathcal{N}(\log \hat r, \Sigma)$ — this matches the
NBLN convention where the latent log-rate is centered at the
gene-level log-mean.

### Closed-form gradient and Hessian via quadrature

Evaluate the Poisson–Beta marginal $\mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{PB}}$
at $K$ fixed Gauss-Legendre quadrature nodes
$\{p_k, w_k\}_{k=1}^K$ on $[0, 1]$ (see @sec-twostate-quadrature
and `scribe.stats._jacobi_quad`). Define the per-node
**Beta-weighted Poisson posterior** over $p$, conditional on $u$
and the current rate $\lambda = \hat r e^{z}$:

$$
q_k(z) =
\frac{w_k \cdot \mathrm{Beta}(p_k \mid \alpha, \beta)\cdot \mathrm{Po}(u \mid \lambda p_k)}
     {\sum_{k'} w_{k'} \cdot \mathrm{Beta}(p_{k'} \mid \alpha, \beta)\cdot \mathrm{Po}(u \mid \lambda p_{k'})}.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-posterior-softmax}

These quadrature softmax weights are the **discrete analogue of
the Beta posterior given the observed count $u$**. The
weighted moments are:

$$
\bar p = \sum_k q_k(z)\, p_k,\qquad
\overline{p^2} = \sum_k q_k(z)\, p_k^2,\qquad
\mathrm{Var}_q(p) = \overline{p^2} - \bar p^{\,2}.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-pmoments}

#### Gradient w.r.t. $z_g$

Differentiate the log-marginal under the integral sign. The
integrand has $\partial \log \mathrm{Po}(u \mid \lambda p) /
\partial z = u - \lambda p$, so:

$$
\frac{\partial \log \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{PB}}}{\partial z_g}
= \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[\frac{\partial \log \mathrm{Po}}{\partial z_g}\right]
= u_g - \lambda_g \,\bar p_g.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-grad}

i.e., observed count minus the posterior-expected Poisson rate.
This is the closed-form analog of NBLN's ``u − (u+r)(1−p)``
gradient (@sec-nb-lognormal-laplace), with ``(u+r)(1−p)``
(the NB conditional posterior mean of the latent Gamma rate)
replaced by $\lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_q[p]$ (the two-state
conditional posterior mean of the Poisson rate).

#### Hessian-diagonal w.r.t. $z_g$

The log-marginal variance identity gives

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \log \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{PB}}}{\partial z_g^2}
= \mathbb{E}_q\!\left[\frac{\partial^2 \log \mathrm{Po}}{\partial z_g^2}\right]
+ \mathrm{Var}_q\!\left(\frac{\partial \log \mathrm{Po}}{\partial z_g}\right).
$$

With $\partial^2 \log \mathrm{Po} / \partial z^2 = -\lambda p$
(second derivative of the Poisson log-PMF in log-rate space)
and $\mathrm{Var}_q(u - \lambda p) = \lambda^2 \mathrm{Var}_q(p)$:

$$
\frac{\partial^2 \log \mathcal{L}_{\mathrm{PB}}}{\partial z_g^2}
= -\lambda_g \bar p_g + \lambda_g^2 \mathrm{Var}_q(p)_g.
$$

Newton uses $-H$, so the Hessian-diagonal entry that enters the
Woodbury solve is:

$$
a_g \;=\; \lambda_g \bar p_g \;-\; \lambda_g^2 \mathrm{Var}_q(p)_g.
$$ {#eq-tsln-rate-hessian-diag}

#### Log-concavity caveat

The Poisson–Beta marginal is **not** uniformly log-concave in
the log-rate. Specifically, when $\alpha < 1$ or $\beta < 1$
the Beta density is U-shaped (not log-concave), and the standard
Prékopa argument for the log-concavity of compound
distributions fails. Empirically, $a_g$ in
@eq-tsln-rate-hessian-diag can be very small or even negative
in the bursty / U-shaped regime.

The implementation defensively clamps $a_g$ at a small positive
floor $\_\text{A\_MIN} = 10^{-3}$ before the Woodbury solve.
Both the raw and clamped versions are returned by the factor
function; clamp activation rate is surfaced on the final result
as `a_clamp_fraction` and `a_clamp_per_gene`. A warning fires
if the clamp activates on more than 5% of `(cell, gene)`
entries on the final sweep.

### Newton step via Woodbury

With $a$ in hand, the per-cell Newton step on
$z_c \in \mathbb{R}^G$ solves

$$
A \delta z = g,\quad A = \mathrm{diag}(a) + \Sigma^{-1},
$$

with $\Sigma = W W^\top + \mathrm{diag}(d)$. Two applications of
the Woodbury identity reduce both the linear solve and the
log-determinant to $\mathcal{O}(Gk + k^3)$ per cell:

$$
A^{-1} = M^{-1} - M^{-1} V S^{-1} V^\top M^{-1},
$$
with $M = \mathrm{diag}(a + 1/d)$, $V = (1/d) \odot W$,
$S = I_k + W^\top M^{-1} W$. This is the same Woodbury
decomposition used by the PLN and NBLN Newton kernels, so
TSLN-Rate inherits NBLN's per-step cost almost exactly (the
only extra work is the $K$-axis quadrature reduction that
computes $\bar p$ and $\mathrm{Var}_q(p)$).

When per-cell capture $\eta_c$ is modelled jointly (biology-
informed prior), the Schur block decomposition
$\delta_\eta = (g_\eta + a^\top A^{-1} g_x) /
(\sum a + 1/\sigma_M^2 - a^\top A^{-1} a)$ adds one extra
solve per Newton step, exactly as in @sec-nb-lognormal-laplace.

## The NB-LogNormal limit {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-nblim}

As $\beta \to \infty$ with $\alpha = r$ fixed and $b = \lambda /
\beta$ finite (the **fast-OFF NB limit** of @sec-twostate), the
Beta–Poisson posterior in @eq-tsln-rate-posterior-softmax
collapses to a Gamma-Poisson conjugate update:

- $\beta \cdot p$ has posterior $\mathrm{Gamma}(r + u, 1 + b)$
  asymptotically.
- $\mathbb{E}_q[p] \to (r + u) / (\beta + \lambda)$.
- $\mathrm{Var}_q(p) \to (r + u) / (\beta + \lambda)^2$.

Plug into the closed forms:

- $\lambda \cdot \mathbb{E}_q[p] \to (r + u) q$ with
  $q = \lambda / (\beta + \lambda)$.
- $\lambda^2 \cdot \mathrm{Var}_q(p) \to (r + u) q^2$.
- $a_g \to (r + u)\, q\, (1 - q)$.

The TSLN-Rate Hessian-diagonal collapses to NBLN's
$a_g = (u + r)\, p_\text{NB}\,(1 - p_\text{NB})$ form with
$p_\text{NB} = 1 - q = \beta/(\beta + \lambda)$. Similarly,
$g_\text{data} \to u - (r + u)\, q$, the NB conditional
gradient. The TSLN-Rate kernel reproduces NBLN's kernel
exactly in the NB limit — confirming that the new model
properly nests NBLN.

The same nesting also gives us a sanity-check assertion in
the test suite: at $\beta = 1000$ and $\alpha = 2$,
$\lambda = 5$, the TSLN-Rate $a_g$ should match NBLN's
$a_g$ to within 5% relative error.

## Identifiability and gauge {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-gauge}

The TSLN-Rate model has a rigid-translation gauge between the
gene-level log-rate centering and the per-cell latent. The
data-side log-likelihood depends on the combined log-rate
$\ell_{cg} = \log \hat r_g + z_{cg} - \eta_c$. The
transformation

$$
\log \hat r_g \to \log \hat r_g + c,\quad
z_{cg} \to z_{cg} - c,\quad
\forall c \in \mathbb{R},
$$

leaves $\ell_{cg}$ unchanged for every cell $c$. The
data-side log-likelihood is invariant; only the Gaussian
prior on $z$ (which penalises shifted $z$) identifies the
gauge — softly. The same gauge structure appears in NBLN
between the latent log-rate prior mean and the per-cell
log-rate (see @sec-diffexp-nbln-rigid-gauge for the full
discussion).

The cascade-and-freeze mechanism resolves this. The SVI
cascade source identifies $\log \hat r_g = \log(\mu_g + b_g
k^-_g)$ tightly from the data — the gene mean is the
single most identifiable per-gene quantity — and a
hard-freeze on $(\mu_g, b_g, k^-_g)$ structurally pins the
gauge direction. The Laplace per-cell $z$ MAP then lives
entirely in the gauge-orthogonal complement.

### Freeze levels

The TSLN-Rate cascade supports four progressively stricter
freeze levels (in plan §7.1 terminology):

- **Level 1 (None)** — all globals M-step optimised; gauge
  softly identified by the $\mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ prior on
  $z$. Not recommended for cascade fits.
- **Level 2 (`mu` soft-cascade)** — $\mu$ has an
  informative-prior loss term; $b, k^-$ unrestricted. Still
  softly identified.
- **Level 3 (`(mu, burst_size, k_off)` soft-cascade)** — all
  three positives have informative-prior loss terms. Gauge
  tightly identified but still soft.
- **Level 4 (`(mu, burst_size, k_off)` hard-frozen)** — all
  three excluded from the optax optimiser. Gauge structurally
  broken. **Default and recommended** for production cascades.

### Gauge contamination ratio

The diagnostic
$\rho_\text{gauge} = \mathrm{std}_c(\bar z_c) /
\mathrm{std}_c(\|z_{c,\perp}\|)$ (with $\bar z_c =
\mathrm{mean}_g(z_{cg})$ the gauge component and $z_{c,\perp}
= z_c - \bar z_c \mathbf{1}$ the gauge-orthogonal residual)
quantifies how much of the per-cell variance lives in the
rigid-translation direction. For a well-frozen Level-4
cascade $\rho_\text{gauge} < 10^{-3}$; for an unfrozen
Level-1 fit $\rho_\text{gauge}$ is typically $\mathcal{O}(1)$.

## Connection to NB-LogNormal {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-nbln-connection}

TSLN-Rate is the natural cascade extension of NB-LogNormal to
the Poisson–Beta compound. The two models share:

- The same per-cell Gaussian latent on log-rates with
  $\Sigma = WW^\top + \mathrm{diag}(d)$.
- The same Woodbury machinery for the per-cell Newton solve.
- The same rigid-translation gauge structure and freeze-level
  hierarchy.
- The same per-cell Laplace approximation around the inner
  MAP.

They differ in the per-gene **shape model**:

- NBLN factors the per-gene over-dispersion as a single
  positive scalar ``r_g``, sufficient for fast-OFF / non-
  bursty genes.
- TSLN-Rate uses the full Poisson–Beta compound from
  @sec-twostate, with three positive parameters
  ``(μ, b, k^-)`` per gene. This captures the bursty /
  bimodal genes the NB structurally cannot represent
  (@sec-twostate § sec-twostate-introduction).

For a dataset where most genes are NB-easy and only a small
fraction are bursty, NBLN can be the better-default fit; for
datasets with a meaningful bursty population (TF-target
modules in many primary tissues), TSLN-Rate's full Poisson–Beta
shape model preserves the bursty signature that NBLN
collapses.

## Practical recommendations {#sec-twostate-tsln-rate-practical}

### Decision tree

- **Independent-gene TwoState** (@sec-twostate) when there is
  no expected cross-gene correlation structure, or when any
  cross-gene structure can be absorbed into per-gene priors.
- **TSLN-Rate** when cross-gene correlation is expected and
  most genes fit the NB regime (the common scRNA-seq case at
  single-cell resolution). The saturation ceiling of TSLN-Logit
  rarely binds at typical molecule counts.
- **NB-LogNormal** when cross-gene correlation is expected and
  the data panel has no bursty / bimodal histograms that the
  NB cannot match.

### Cascade workflow

1. Fit upstream `TwoState`-SVI to convergence on the full
   gene panel.
2. Cascade with default Level-4 freeze
   (`("mu", "burst_size", "k_off")` hard-frozen).
3. Diagnose the gauge contamination ratio
   (`std_c(mean_g(z_cg))` / `std_c(||z_c − mean_g(z_c) · 1||)`)
   on the post-fit `result.x_loc − result.mu[None, :]`. It
   should be below $10^{-2}$ on real data; smaller is
   better.
4. Optionally relax freezes via $\tau > 1$ to let the
   Laplace fit refine the gene-level positives on top of
   the SVI source's MAP.

### Reading the result

`ScribeLaplaceResults` for `twostate_ln_rate` populates the
following fields:

- `mu` — the **latent log-rate prior center** (= $\log
  \hat r$). Real-valued, shape $(G,)$. This is what the
  `LowRankMultivariateNormal(loc=mu, …)` distribution for
  `y_log_rate` uses; same convention as NBLN/PLN. **Not** the
  TwoState positive gene-mean parameter.
- `gene_mean` — the positive TwoState gene-mean parameter
  (= the SVI source's `mu`). User-facing biology.
- `burst_size`, `k_off` — the other two positive
  TwoState gene-level parameters.
- `alpha`, `beta`, `r_hat` — derived from the three
  positives via the mean-preserving floor of
  @_twostate_reparam.
- `x_loc` — per-cell latent log-rate MAP, shape $(C, G)$.
- `eta_loc` — per-cell capture offset MAP (or None).
- `gene_mean_loc`, `gene_mean_scale`,
  `burst_size_loc`, `burst_size_scale`,
  `k_off_loc`, `k_off_scale` — unconstrained
  posterior-Normal location/scale for each gene-level
  positive (set to None for frozen parameters; populated
  via the diagonal-Hessian Laplace approximation for
  unfrozen ones).
- `W`, `d` — low-rank loadings and diagonal residual
  variance (the prior covariance structure
  $\Sigma = WW^\top + \mathrm{diag}(d)$).
- `a_raw_min`, `a_raw_negative_fraction`,
  `a_clamp_fraction`, `a_clamp_per_gene` — curvature-clamp
  diagnostics (see @sec-twostate-tsln-rate-laplace).

### Computational cost

The per-cell Newton inner loop has cost
$\mathcal{O}(G K + Gk + k^3)$ per iterate, with $K$ the
quadrature node count and $k$ the latent rank. Empirically
this is roughly 1.5× NB-LogNormal's per-step time on
matched configurations (the extra factor is the $K$-axis
quadrature reduction that NBLN's closed-form
$a_g = (u+r)p(1-p)$ avoids). Both scale linearly in $G$
and $C$ at fixed $k$ and $K$.

### Validation signal

The most important "is the latent doing real work"
diagnostic is the **cross-gene mean covariance** of the
post-fit per-cell latent. Specifically, compute
$\mathrm{Cov}_c(x_c - \mu)$ across cells and inspect the
off-diagonal entries. If they are near-zero, the latent is
silently behaving like the independent-gene TwoState model
— which would indicate either insufficient training, a
configuration error, or a bug. On well-conditioned data
with genuine cross-gene structure, off-diagonal entries
should be at the same order of magnitude as the diagonal.

# Cross-gene correlation through activation log-odds (TSLN-Logit) {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit}

TSLN-Rate (@sec-twostate-tsln-rate-model) attaches the
per-cell Gaussian latent to the ON-production rate
$\hat r_{cg} = \hat r_g \cdot e^{z_{cg}}$. The conditional
mean responds multiplicatively: $\mathbb{E}[u_{cg}\mid
z_{cg}] = \hat r_g \, e^{z_{cg}} \,
\alpha_g/(\alpha_g+\beta_g)$. There is no biophysical
upper bound on $\mathbb{E}[u \mid z]$ as $z \to \infty$.

The alternative — attach the latent to the **activation
log-odds** $\theta_g = \log(k^+_g / k^-_g)$ — has a
different, complementary structure. The conditional mean
becomes
$\mathbb{E}[u_{cg} \mid z_{cg}] = \mathrm{rate}_g \cdot
\sigma(\theta_g + z_{cg})$, which **saturates at $\mathrm{rate}_g$**
in the limit $z \to \infty$ and decays to zero in the
$z \to -\infty$ limit. Biophysically, this matches the
Boltzmann picture: a TF or chromatin perturbation shifts
the activation free-energy $\Delta G_\mathrm{act} = -k_BT \log(k^+/k^-)$
linearly, and the steady-state ON-fraction follows the
Boltzmann sigmoid.

This section develops TSLN-Logit (Variant B in the plan)
in the same depth as TSLN-Rate.

## TSLN-Logit generative model {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-model}

Reparameterise the two-state model at the gene level in
terms of three quantities that are natural for the
activation-log-odds latent:

- $\mathrm{rate}_g \in \mathbb{R}_+$ : gene-level
  ON-production rate. **Crucially $z$-independent** — this
  is the property that makes the cross-gene mean
  covariance non-zero (see @sec-twostate-tsln-logit-why-fixed-rate).
- $\kappa_g \in \mathbb{R}_+$ : Beta concentration
  $\kappa_g = \alpha_g + \beta_g$.
- $\theta_g \in \mathbb{R}$ : per-gene activation log-odds
  $\theta_g = \log(\alpha_g/\beta_g) = \log(k^+_g/k^-_g)$.

These map back to the natural parameterisation via
$\alpha_g = \kappa_g\,\sigma(\theta_g)$ and
$\beta_g = \kappa_g\,(1-\sigma(\theta_g))$ at $z = 0$.

**Per cell ``c``**, draw a Gaussian latent (zero-centred):

$$
z_c \sim \mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, \Sigma),\quad
\Sigma = W W^\top + \mathrm{diag}(d),
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-latent-prior}

with the same low-rank structure as TSLN-Rate. The
**activation log-odds** is then shifted per (cell, gene):

$$
\eta_{\mathrm{act},cg} = \theta_g + z_{cg},\quad
\varphi_{cg} = \sigma(\eta_{\mathrm{act},cg}),
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-eta-act}

so the per-cell Beta shape parameters are
$\alpha_{cg} = \kappa_g \varphi_{cg}$ and
$\beta_{cg} = \kappa_g (1 - \varphi_{cg})$. The
observation is a Poisson–Beta compound:

$$
p_{cg} \sim \mathrm{Beta}(\alpha_{cg}, \beta_{cg}),\quad
u_{cg} \mid p_{cg}, z_c \sim
\mathrm{Poisson}(\nu_c \, \mathrm{rate}_g \, p_{cg}),
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-obs}

with $\nu_c = e^{-\eta_{\mathrm{cap},c}}$ for optional
fixed-offset capture (see @sec-twostate-tsln-logit-capture).

### Why ``rate`` must be gene-level {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-why-fixed-rate}

The defining property of TSLN-Logit is that the
conditional mean $\mathbb{E}[u_{cg} \mid z_{cg}]$
depends on $z$ through the sigmoid. Computing the
cross-gene mean covariance using the law of total
covariance,

$$
\mathrm{Cov}(u_g, u_h) =
\underbrace{\mathrm{Cov}\!\big(\mathbb{E}[u_g \mid z],\,
                                \mathbb{E}[u_h \mid z]\big)}_{\text{mean covariance}}
\;+\;
\underbrace{\mathbb{E}\!\big[\mathrm{Cov}(u_g, u_h \mid z)\big]}_{\text{conditional covariance}}
,
$$

the mean covariance is the off-diagonal signal we want and
the conditional covariance is zero (independent per-gene
Poisson–Beta given $z$).  Substituting the conditional
means,

$$
\mathrm{Cov}\big(\mathrm{rate}_g\,\sigma(\theta_g + z_g),\,
                  \mathrm{rate}_h\,\sigma(\theta_h + z_h)\big)
= \mathrm{rate}_g\,\mathrm{rate}_h \,
  \mathrm{Cov}\!\big(\sigma(\theta_g + z_g),\,
                      \sigma(\theta_h + z_h)\big),
$$

which is non-zero whenever $\Sigma_{gh} \neq 0$.

The first draft of this model (Rev 0 in the plan) made
$\mathrm{rate}_g$ a function of $z$ as well, with the
intent of preserving the gene-level mean
$\mathbb{E}[u_{cg} \mid z_{cg}] = \mu_g$ exactly. The
audit caught a fatal flaw: with $\mathbb{E}[u_g \mid z] =
\mu_g$ constant in $z$, the mean covariance collapses to
$\mathrm{Cov}(\mu_g, \mu_h) = 0$ — the entire cross-gene
signal Variant B was supposed to add disappears. **Rev 4
fixes this by holding $\mathrm{rate}_g$ at the gene level
unconditionally**, which gives up the exact mean
preservation but restores the cross-gene mean covariance.
A regression test in
``tests/test_twostate_ln_logit_recovery.py`` pins this
invariant.

The user-facing reporting mean at $z = 0$ is
$\bar\mu_g = \mathrm{rate}_g \cdot \sigma(\theta_g)$, which
is what the result object's ``gene_mean`` field carries.

## Per-cell Laplace marginalization {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-laplace}

The per-cell joint density is

$$
\log p(u_c, z_c \mid \Theta) =
\sum_g \log L_{\mathrm{PB}}\big(u_{cg} \mid
                                 \kappa_g \sigma(\theta_g + z_{cg}),\,
                                 \kappa_g(1 - \sigma(\theta_g + z_{cg})),\,
                                 \mathrm{rate}_g\big)
- \tfrac{1}{2}(z_c)^\top \Sigma^{-1} z_c,
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-joint-density}

with $L_{\mathrm{PB}}$ the Poisson–Beta marginal from
@sec-twostate-poisson-beta. The latent prior is centred at
zero because the gene baseline is captured by $\theta_g$;
this is the structural difference from TSLN-Rate, where
the latent prior centre was $\log \hat r_g$.

### Closed-form gradient via $\varphi'$

The integrand at quadrature node $p_k$ is
$T_k(z) = w_k \cdot \mathrm{Beta}(p_k \mid
\kappa\sigma(\theta+z), \kappa(1-\sigma(\theta+z)))
\cdot \mathrm{Poisson}(u \mid \mathrm{rate}\,p_k)$. Only
the Beta-density piece depends on $z$ (the Poisson rate is
$z$-independent), so

$$
\frac{\partial \log T_k}{\partial z}
= \varphi'(\theta+z) \cdot D_k,
\qquad
D_k = \kappa\big[\log p_k - \log(1-p_k) -
                 \big(\psi(\kappa\varphi) - \psi(\kappa(1-\varphi))\big)\big],
$$

with $\varphi'(x) = \sigma(x)(1-\sigma(x))$ the sigmoid
derivative. Taking $\mathbb{E}_q[\cdot]$ over the posterior
softmax weights $q_k = \mathrm{softmax}_k[\log T_k]$ gives
the data-side gradient on $z$:

$$
g_{\mathrm{data},g} = \varphi'_g \cdot \mathbb{E}_q[D]_g.
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-g-data}

### Three-term Hessian-diagonal

Using the log-marginal identity
$\partial^2 \log L / \partial z^2 =
\mathbb{E}_q[\partial^2 \log T / \partial z^2] +
\mathrm{Var}_q[\partial \log T / \partial z]$ and the fact
that $\partial D/\partial\varphi = -\kappa^2[\psi'(\kappa\varphi) +
\psi'(\kappa(1-\varphi))]$ is constant in $p$, the data-side
Hessian-diagonal factor (with sign convention $a = -H$) is

$$
\boxed{
a_g =
-\varphi''_g \cdot \mathbb{E}_q[D]_g
+\varphi'^2_g \cdot \kappa^2_g \, \big(\psi'(\kappa_g \varphi_g) +
                                          \psi'(\kappa_g(1-\varphi_g))\big)
-\varphi'^2_g \cdot \kappa^2_g \, \mathrm{Var}_q\!\big(\log p - \log(1-p)\big).
}
$$ {#eq-tsln-logit-a}

Three terms with distinct sign behaviour:

- $-\varphi'' \mathbb{E}_q[D]$ has sign that depends on
  both factors. Vanishes at $\varphi = \tfrac{1}{2}$.
- $+\varphi'^2 \kappa^2 (\psi' + \psi')$ is **always
  positive** (trigamma is positive).
- $-\varphi'^2 \kappa^2 \mathrm{Var}_q[\log p - \log(1-p)]$
  is **always non-positive**.

Like TSLN-Rate, the Poisson–Beta marginal in $z$ is
**not** uniformly log-concave when the Beta is U-shaped
($\alpha < 1$ or $\beta < 1$). The implementation
defensively clamps $a_g$ at $a_{\min} = 10^{-3}$ before the
Woodbury solve; the post-fit ``a_clamp_fraction`` diagnostic
surfaces how often the clamp activated.

A subtle implementation point worth recording: in
pathological iterates the raw sigmoid can underflow to 0
or saturate to 1 in float32. The factor function clips
$\varphi$ to $[10^{-6}, 1-10^{-6}]$ before evaluating
$\varphi'$, $\varphi''$, and the digamma / trigamma terms.
The derivative formulas then use the **clipped** $\varphi$,
so they are *pseudo-derivatives* of the true clipped
likelihood outside the clip region. This is intentional:
$\varphi' = 0$ at the unclipped boundary would freeze the
Newton step (no first-order information), whereas the
clipped formulae act as a Newton stabiliser that keeps
the iterate moving. In normal regimes the clip is inactive
and the formulae are exact.

## Identifiability and gauge {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-gauge}

TSLN-Logit has the same kind of rigid-translation gauge as
TSLN-Rate, but here the gauge is **exact** in a way TSLN-
Rate's only approximated.

The data-side log-likelihood depends on $\eta_{\mathrm{act},cg}
= \theta_g + z_{cg}$ alone (the Beta shape parameters are
functions of $\eta_{\mathrm{act}}$, and $\mathrm{rate}_g$ is
$z$-independent). The transformation

$$
\theta_g \to \theta_g + c, \quad z_{cg} \to z_{cg} - c,
\quad \forall c \in \mathbb{R}, \forall g \in \{1, \dots, G\},
$$

leaves $\eta_{\mathrm{act},cg}$ — and hence the data-side
log-likelihood — invariant exactly. (Contrast with TSLN-
Rate, where shifting $\log \hat r$ and $z$ in opposite
directions preserves the rate but the Beta shape parameters
$(\alpha, \beta)$ remain pinned to their gene-level values,
so the gauge applied at the *log-rate* level rather than at
the Beta shape level.) Only the Gaussian prior on $z$
identifies the gauge — softly.

### Freeze levels

The same four-level cascade hierarchy applies as for TSLN-
Rate (with the gene-level parameter set adjusted for
Variant B):

- **Level 1 (None)** — all globals M-step optimised; gauge
  softly identified by the $\mathcal{N}(0, \Sigma)$ prior on
  $z$. Not recommended.
- **Level 2 (`eta_anchor` soft-cascade)** — $\theta$ has an
  informative-prior loss term; $\mathrm{rate}, \kappa$
  unrestricted. Gauge tightly identified, soft.
- **Level 3 (`(rate, eta_anchor)` hard-frozen, `kappa`
  soft)** — both rigid-axis-relevant globals frozen;
  $\kappa$ is the only learnable gene-level global. Use
  when the SVI source may have biased $\kappa$.
- **Level 4 (`(rate, kappa, eta_anchor)` all hard-frozen)**
  — all gene-level globals pinned at the SVI MAP; only
  $(W, d)$ learned. **Default and recommended** for
  production cascades.

The Rev 3 audit reasoning for promoting $\kappa$ into the
default freeze: $\kappa$ controls Beta concentration /
overdispersion; leaving it soft-cascade would let the
Laplace fit absorb correlation / model mismatch into
$\kappa$ rather than into $(W, d)$ where the cross-gene
signal belongs.

### Gauge contamination ratio

The same diagnostic $\rho_{\mathrm{gauge}} =
\mathrm{std}_c(\bar z_c) /
\mathrm{std}_c(\|z_{c,\perp}\|)$ applies. For a fully
converged Level-4 fit $\rho_{\mathrm{gauge}} < 10^{-3}$.
The cascade-frozen recovery tests in
``tests/test_twostate_ln_logit_gauge.py`` verify this
behaviour on short test-fits.

## Capture {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-capture}

The biology-anchored capture prior used by NBLN and
TSLN-Rate (a TruncN on $\eta_{\mathrm{cap},c}$ with
biology-informed $(\log M_0, \sigma_M)$) requires the
joint Newton on $(z_c, \eta_{\mathrm{cap},c})$ with the
cross-Hessian
$H_{z,\eta_{\mathrm{cap}}}$. **For TSLN-Logit the cross-
Hessian does NOT reduce to the NBLN / TSLN-Rate shortcut.**
In Variant A the variables enter the same scalar log-rate
$\ell = \log \hat r + z - \eta_{\mathrm{cap}}$ with
opposite signs, so $\partial / \partial \eta_{\mathrm{cap}}
= -\partial / \partial z$ on the data side and the cross-
block reduces to $-a_g$. In Variant B the same symmetry
does NOT hold: $z$ enters the Beta density only (via
$\alpha, \beta$), while $\eta_{\mathrm{cap}}$ enters the
Poisson log-rate only. They are perturbing different parts
of the integrand. The correct cross-Hessian is

$$
H_{z_g, \eta_{\mathrm{cap}},c} =
\varphi'_g \cdot \lambda_g \cdot \kappa_g \,
  \mathrm{Cov}_q\!\big(\log p - \log(1-p),\, p\big),
$$

which is the same posterior-covariance term that appears in
the diagonal Hessian decomposition.

The audit-recommended fallback for the initial release
(PR-2 of the plan) is to **disable the joint capture
Newton path entirely** for TSLN-Logit. Two capture modes
are supported:

- **No capture** ($\nu_c \equiv 1$): the Newton kernel
  optimises $z$ only. Routes to the ``x_only`` path.
- **Fixed-offset capture** ($\eta_{\mathrm{cap},c}$ pinned
  at the upstream MAP): $\nu_c = e^{-\eta_{\mathrm{cap},c}}$
  is a stop-gradient constant on the Poisson log-rate.
  Routes to the ``x_only_offset`` path. The kernel solves
  for $z$ alone; the prior on $\eta_{\mathrm{cap}}$ is not
  evaluated (the offset is treated as observed).

The biology-anchored capture path with proper joint
Newton is deferred to a phase-3 follow-up. In practice the
fixed-offset mode covers the production use case — most
cascades route through an SVI source that already fit
capture per cell, and the cascade adapter pins those
values automatically (the run-inference stage auto-adds
``"eta"`` to ``freeze_params`` when the SVI source has
per-cell capture).

## When to use TSLN-Logit vs TSLN-Rate {#sec-twostate-tsln-logit-when}

Both Variants A and B share the cascade machinery, the
Woodbury per-cell Newton, the Level-4 freeze convention,
and the post-fit profiled-Hessian uncertainty. They
differ in **how the latent enters the data** and therefore
in the shape of the conditional mean response:

| | TSLN-Rate (A) | TSLN-Logit (B) |
|---|---|---|
| Latent enters at | log-rate ($\log\hat r + z$) | activation log-odds ($\theta + z$) |
| $\mathbb{E}[u \mid z]$ as $z \to \infty$ | unbounded | saturates at $\mathrm{rate}_g$ |
| Cross-gene mean cov source | $\mathrm{Cov}(e^{z_g}, e^{z_h})$ | $\mathrm{Cov}(\sigma(\theta_g + z_g), \sigma(\theta_h + z_h))$ |
| NB-limit (β → ∞) | NB-LogNormal exactly | linear in $z$ in the NB tail |
| Gauge structure | rigid-translation between $\log\hat r$ and $z$ (data-likelihood invariant) | rigid-translation between $\theta$ and $z$ (**exact**) |
| Capture support (PR-2) | x-only / fixed-offset / soft / joint | x-only / fixed-offset only |
| Biophysical interpretation | log-rate fluctuation | Boltzmann free-energy fluctuation on activation odds |

**Use TSLN-Rate** when the expected cross-gene signal is
on the gene expression *level* (TF-driven amplification,
broadly co-varying programmes) and you want unbounded
mean response. Easier to interpret as a direct extension
of NB-LogNormal.

**Use TSLN-Logit** when the expected signal is on
*activation state* (chromatin-mediated regulons, on/off
program switches), when saturation at a biophysical
ceiling is plausible, or when the data is bimodal /
bursty enough that the sigmoid's saturation captures the
observed mean response better than an exponential.

The two are not mutually exclusive at the dataset level —
running both and comparing the ELBO / cross-gene
covariance signal is a sensible diagnostic when the right
choice is not obvious from the biology.
